perturbation.original_text: Borrower shall use the Information solely in its credit, risk management and net capital processes, including, without limitation, functions such as concentration limit monitor ing, compliance or legal review, or senior management oversight as Borrower may reasonably deem necessary to comply wit h regulatory requirements including, without limitation, regulatory capital computation, regulatory reporting and concentration limit monitoring .
perturbation.changed_text: Borrower shall endeavor to use the Information in its credit, risk management and net capital processes, including functions such as concentration limit monitoring, compliance or legal review, or senior management oversight as Borrower may consider appropriate to comply with regulatory requirements including regulatory capital computation, regulatory reporting and concentration limit monitoring.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall use solely' to 'shall endeavor to use' and 'may reasonably deem necessary' to 'may consider appropriate' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to use the information specifically for regulatory compliance. This creates uncertainty about whether the borrower is strictly adhering to regulatory requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 17a-4 requires broker-dealers to maintain and preserve accurate records to ensure regulatory compliance and oversight. By weakening the obligation to use information specifically for regulatory compliance, the modified text could lead to inadequate record-keeping practices, hindering the SEC's ability to monitor and enforce securities regulations. The rule necessitates broker-dealers diligently use information related to their risk management and net capital processes for compliance and reporting, and 'endeavoring' to do so, rather than a strict requirement, undermines the integrity of required record-keeping and reporting.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.17a-4 Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. This section applies to the following types of entities: A member of a national securities exchange who transacts a business in securities directly with others than members of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer who transacts a business in securities through the medium of a member of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer as that term is defined in § 240.3b-12 , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o ); a security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o -10) that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. Section 240.18a-6 (rather than this section) applies to the following types of entities: A security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. (a) Every member , broker or dealer subject to § 240.17a-3 must preserve for a period of not less than 6 years, the first two years
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall use solely' to 'shall endeavor to use' and 'may reasonably deem necessary' to 'may consider appropriate' weakens the legal obligation for the borrower to strictly adhere to regulatory requirements for record keeping. SEC Rule 17a-4, as the Law Explanation describes, mandates broker-dealers maintain and preserve accurate records. While the provided snippets do not explicitly state the exact wording of the record-keeping requirement, they confirm the existence of 17 CFR 240.17a-4, which pertains to 'Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers'. By introducing ambiguity, the changed text now contradicts the intent of the rule, creating uncertainty and potentially leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Borrower shall limit the distribution of the Information within the firm to only those credit, risk managemen t, regulatory reporting and compliance areas who perform the functions described above. In particular, Borrower shall not permit the trading desks within the firm to have access to the Information, except and only to the extent that such trading desks are involved in the ordinary course of business in risk management functions.
perturbation.changed_text: Borrower should attempt to limit the distribution of the Information within the firm to credit, risk management, regulatory reporting and compliance areas. The trading desks within the firm should generally not have access to the Information, except to the extent they are involved in risk management functions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall limit' to 'should attempt' and 'shall not permit' to 'should generally not' makes the restriction on information distribution less stringent and more open to interpretation. This weakens the protection of confidential information and could potentially allow unauthorized access.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation S-P mandates the protection of customer information by financial institutions. The original text ensures limited access, however, the modified text weakens this obligation, creating the potential for non-compliance with privacy regulations, thereby contradicting the core intent of Regulation S-P to protect nonpublic personal information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 248.30 - Procedures to safeguard customer information, including response programs for unauthorized access to customer information and customer notice; disposal of customer information and consumer information. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 248.30 - Procedures to safeguard customer information, including response programs for unauthorized access to customer information and customer notice; disposal of customer information and consumer information. CFR Table of Popular Names § 248.30 Procedures to safeguard customer information, including response programs for unauthorized access to customer information and customer notice; disposal of customer information and consumer information. (a) Policies and procedures to safeguard customer information —(1) General requirements. Every covered institution must develop, implement, and maintain written policies and procedures that address administrative, technical, and physical safeguards for the protection of customer information. (2) Objectives. These written policies and procedures must be reasonably designed to: (i) Ensure the security and confidentiality of customer information; (ii) Protect against any anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of customer information; and (iii) Protect against unauthorized access to or use of customer information that could result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any customer . (3) Response programs for unauthorized access to or use of customer information. Written policies and procedures in paragraph (a)(1) of this section must include a program reasonably designed to detect, respond to, and recover from unauthorized access to or use of customer information, including customer notification procedures . This response program must include procedures for the covered institution to: (i) Assess the nature and scope of any incident involving unauthorized acc
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect customer information, creating a potential contradiction with 17 CFR § 248.30. Specifically, changing 'shall limit' to 'should attempt to limit' and 'shall not permit' to 'should generally not' introduces ambiguity, making it harder to enforce the restriction on information distribution. The original text ensures limited access aligning with (a)(2)(i) 'Ensure the security and confidentiality of customer information', while the modified text creates the potential for non-compliance and unauthorized access, undermining (a)(2)(iii) 'Protect against unauthorized access to or use of customer information that could result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any customer'.

perturbation.original_text: Borrower shall maintain the Information in confidence and shall not disclose the information to any third party with out the consent of Lending Agent; provided, however, that Borrower may disclose the Information to the extent required by Sample 2 subpoena, court order, request from a regulatory body with jurisdiction over Borrower, or as otherwise required by law or regulation.
perturbation.changed_text: Borrower will try to maintain the Information in confidence and will generally not disclose the information to any third party without the Lending Agent's consent; however, Borrower may disclose the Information if required by subpoena, court order, request from a regulatory body, or as required by law or regulation.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain' to 'will try to maintain' and 'shall not disclose' to 'will generally not disclose' weakens the confidentiality obligation, making it less enforceable. The addition of 'try' introduces an element of discretion in maintaining confidentiality, potentially conflicting with legal standards for protecting sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA) requires financial institutions to protect the confidentiality of customer information. By changing the language to 'will try to maintain,' the agreement introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. This contradicts the GLBA, which mandates a higher standard of care for protecting sensitive financial information.

Obligations with respect to disclosures of personal information (a) Notice requirements Except as otherwise provided in this subchapter, a financial institution may not, directly or through any affiliate, disclose to a nonaffiliated third party any nonpublic personal information, unless such financial institution provid
perturbation.justification: The original contract language uses 'shall maintain' and 'shall not disclose,' creating a clear obligation. The modified version, using 'will try to maintain' and 'will generally not disclose,' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA), as mentioned in the Law Explanation, mandates a high standard of care for protecting sensitive financial information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 6802, discusses obligations regarding disclosures of personal information, stating that 'a financial institution may not...disclose to a nonaffiliated third party any nonpublic personal information'. The weakening of the language contradicts the intent of maintaining confidentiality as outlined in GLBA and weakens the obligation outlined in 15 USC 6802. The change creates a contradiction because it replaces a mandatory obligation with a discretionary one, potentially failing legal scrutiny related to data protection.

perturbation.original_text: If Borrower (or its employees or agents) breaches or threatens to breach this Agreement, Lending Agent may, in addi tion to any other remedies available, seek any equitable remedy it deems appropriate to enjoin an actual or threatened violation by Borrower.
perturbation.changed_text: If Borrower (or its employees or agents) breaches or threatens to breach this Agreement, Lending Agent may, if they find it suitable, seek an equitable remedy to enjoin an actual or threatened violation by Borrower.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'if they find it suitable' introduces ambiguity and discretion in the decision to seek legal remedy, weakening the lending agent's ability to enforce the agreement's terms. This makes the availability of remedies less certain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR § 6301 outlines the grounds for preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders. By inserting the phrase 'if they find it suitable', the amended clause weakens the Lending Agent's right to seek an injunction for breaches of contract, which conflicts with the legal availability of injunctive relief as outlined in the CPLR, which provides for injunctive relief when specific conditions are met.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.

A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had. perturbation.justification: The addition of "if they find it suitable" weakens the Lending Agent's ability to seek an injunction, introducing ambiguity and discretion. This contradicts the intent of New York CPLR § 6301, which outlines the grounds for preliminary injunctions, effectively providing a mechanism for injunctive relief when specific conditions are met. By making the pursuit of an equitable remedy discretionary, the changed text undermines the legal right to seek an injunction for breaches, as outlined in the scraped law snippets, which detail the grounds for preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders under NY CPLR § 6301. Both snippets confirm that a preliminary injunction may be granted when a defendant violates the plaintiff's rights, but the modified contract introduces an element of subjective suitability, weakening the enforceability of this right.
